coredata.dc:title: Solution-processed carbon dots based cathode interlayer for high efficiency inverted organic solar cells with improved photostability
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Xia
Li, Yuting
Cui, Mengqi
Wang, Yuying
Hao, Xiujuan
Zhang, Yangyang
Li, Na
Chen, Yan
Gao, Xingsen
Rong, Qikun
Nian, Li
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dots (C-dots) with amino groups are synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method, which could be treated in aqueous solution. The new material is used as cathode interlayer in organic solar cells (OSCs) for the first time. The C-dots cathode interlayer reduces the work function, improves the extraction and transportation of charge carriers, and reduces the recombination of charge carriers in device. As a result, the champion power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the C-dots based device is 15.41% when using PM6:Y6 as active layer. In addition, after 1 h of continuous UV illumination, 97.5% of the original PCE for C-dots based device could be maintained while devices based on ZnO cathode interlayer only remained 80.9%, indicating that C-dots are potential candidates as stable and efficient cathode interlayer to overcome the inherent poor photostability drawback of ZnO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cathode interlayer
Organic solar cells
Photostability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The molecular structures of Erythosin B and PEI. (b) HRTEM image and SAED (inset) pattern of C-dots. (c) FTIR spectrum of C-dots, Erythosin B and PEI. (d) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots.

(a) J–V characteristics of the devices with different cathode interlayers under AM 1.5G illumination at 100 mW cm−2. (b) EQE curves for the devices. (c) J–V characteristics of devices with different cathode interlayers in dark.

(a) Dark J–V characteristics for ZnO-based device before and after illumination for 1 h in inert atmosphere. (b) Dark J–V characteristics for C-dots-based device before and after illumination for 1 h. (c) The evolution of J–V curves for ZnO-based device under UV illumination for 1 h. (d) The evolution of J–V curves for C-dots-based device under UV illumination for 1 h. (e) Normalized JSC, VOC, FF, and PCE of ZnO-based device under continuous UV illumination for 1 h. (f) Normalized JSC, VOC, FF, and PCE of C-dots-based device under continuous UV illumination for 1 h.
Photovoltaic parameters for devices with different electron transport layer under simulated AM 1.5G illumination at 100 mW cm−2.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average PCEs in brackets are based on 15 devices.

head.ce:title: Solution-processed carbon dots based cathode interlayer for high efficiency inverted organic solar cells with improved photostability

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Optical Information Materials and Technology & Institute for Advanced Materials, South China Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon dots (C-dots) with amino groups are synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method, which could be treated in aqueous solution. The new material is used as cathode interlayer in organic solar cells (OSCs) for the first time. The C-dots cathode interlayer reduces the work function, improves the extraction and transportation of charge carriers, and reduces the recombination of charge carriers in device. As a result, the champion power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the C-dots based device is 15.41% when using PM6:Y6 as active layer. In addition, after 1 h of continuous UV illumination, 97.5% of the original PCE for C-dots based device could be maintained while devices based on ZnO cathode interlayer only remained 80.9%, indicating that C-dots are potential candidates as stable and efficient cathode interlayer to overcome the inherent poor photostability drawback of ZnO.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic solar cells (OSCs) are considered as promising candidates for clean energy due to its unique features of low cost, mechanical flexibility, and solution processability [1–3]. To date, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) for single-junction organic photovoltaic cells has reached the 19% milestone，taking a step further in the industrial production and applications of OSCs [4]. PCE, as the most important device photovoltaic parameter, can be further improved by synthetic design of novel photoactive materials [5,6], morphology optimization and interface engineering [7–9]. For producing efficient and stable OSCs, cathode interlayer between active layer and cathode plays a key role due to their fundamental importance to the electron extraction process [10].

ZnO is the most commonly used cathode interlayer material due to its high transparency, high electron mobility, solubility-treatable and suitable energy levels [11–13]. However, it usually requires the high-temperature annealing post-treatment, which leads to incompatibility with printable or flexible substrates. Additionally, as a well-known photo-catalyst for organic compound, ZnO would facilitate the decomposition of non-fullerene acceptors under UV irradiation, resulting in photo instability issues for devices [14]. Recently, organic interlayer materials have been widely used in OSCs, including polymeric materials PFN-Br, polyethylenimine (PEI) and polyethylenimine ethoxylated (PEIE), and small-molecule materials PDINO and PDINN [15–19]. These materials usually contain ammonium salts or ammonia groups, which could reduce the work function (WF) of the cathode by forming an interface dipole to shift the vacuum level [20]. However, due to the solubility and adverse reactions at the interface [21], these organic interface materials are mainly used in fullerene based OSCs or non-fullerene based OSCs with conventional device architecture. Therefore, efficient and stable cathode interlayer that could be used in non-fullerene based inverted OSCs (i-OSCs) is still quite scarce and need to be solved urgently.

As an emerging carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (C-dots) have the advantages of efficient synthetic route, low cost, low toxicity and excellent optical properties, which could be used for stable and environmentally friendly biomedical applications, sensors and optoelectronic devices. Huang et al. used carbon dots as the down-conversion layer to convert near-ultraviolet light and blue-violet light in the incident light into visible light with higher absorption efficiency in the active layer, which improved the device performance [22]. Yang et al. synthesized carbon dots and used them as the cathode interlayer in i-OSCs for the first time [23]. So far, most cathode interlayer based on carbon-based materials, such as C-dots and graphene quantum dots, have low device performance compared with ZnO or organic interlayer. It is mainly due to the fact that the currently used carbon-based interface materials are highly carbonized, resulting in insufficient surface modification groups (such as amine groups) to achieve sufficient reduction of WF and enhancement of carrier extraction.

Herein, amino groups functionalized C-dots were prepared by a simple hydrothermal reaction from PEI and Erythrosin B, which were used as cathode interlayer for efficient and stable i-OSCs. Compared to ZnO cathode interlayer, C-dots cathode interlayer showed improved charge carrier extraction and better device performance. The device with C-dots cathode interlayer exhibited PCE of 15.41%, with an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.83 V, a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 24.52 mA cm−2, and a fill factor (FF) of 75.89%. Furthermore, the C-dots cathode interface layer significantly improved the photostability of OSCs. After under continuous UV illumination (provided by a UV lamp at 5 mW cm−2) for 1 h, the C-dots-based OSCs maintained 97.5% of its initial performance, while devices based on ZnO cathode interlayer only remained 80.9%.

The C-dots were hydrothermally synthesized with Erythrosin B and PEI as the precursors. The chemical structure of Erythosin B and PEI were shown in Fig. 1 a. In detail, 13 mg of Erythrosin B and 1 mL of PEI were dissolved in 4 mL deionized water and mixed thoroughly. The mixture was then transferred into a 10 mL Teflon-lined, stainless steel autoclave and placed in an oven at 160 °C for 6 h. The dark brown and yellow solution obtained was purified by dialysis (membrane molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da). Finally, the carbon dots were stored at 4 °C in the dark. It is notably that the new material is easy to synthesize and inexpensive. Due to the amino groups, the C-dots are soluble in aqueous solution, preventing the use of toxic organic solvents.

Firstly, we investigated the morphology and particle size of the C-dots using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Fig. S1a). It can be found that C-dots have a spherical shape with relatively uniform size. According to the size distribution histogram (Fig. S1b), the particle size of C-dots was mainly distributed from 3 nm to 6 nm. Fig. 1b illustrated the typical high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of C-dots and the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset in Fig. 1b). The crystal lattice spacing of C-dots was about 0.16 nm. X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS; Fig. S2) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1c) were performed to understand the chemical and structural composition of C-dots. It can be seen that the C-dots were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. As shown in Fig. S2c, the spike of N peak was clearly visible at around 400 eV, and was divided into two peak centers at 398.4 eV, 398.85 eV, corresponding to the C–NH2, C–N–C groups, respectively. The chemical groups of the C-dots were shown in Fig. 1c. About 3300 cm−1 peak can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the N–H bond, and the stretching vibration peak of the C–N bond can also be seen around 1200 cm−1. Above all, the dates and results indicated that the C-dots were functionalized with amine groups. Previous studies have shown that the charge transfer of the amine group to the cathode forms the interface dipole between the interlayer and the cathode, thereby reducing the cathode work function and improving the built-in electric field in the OSCs, and finally realizing the efficient carrier extraction [24]. The optical properties of the C-dots were characterized by the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra. The C-dots solution had an absorption peak at 490 nm and a narrow fluorescence emission peak at 516 nm (Fig. 1d).

The devices based on different cathode interlayers (ZnO, C-dots and PEI) were fabricated with device structure: ITO/cathode interlayer/PM6:Y6/MoO3/Al. The chemical structure of PM6 and Y6 were shown in Fig. S3. The detail fabricating process of OSCs was shown in Supporting Information. Fig. 2 a illustrated the best device performances of OSCs with different cathode interlayers under simulated AM 1.5G illumination at 100 mW cm−2. The detailed photovoltaic parameters were listed in Table 1 . The device based on ZnO cathode interlayer showed PCEmax of 14.79%, where Voc was 0.83 V, Jsc was 24.26 mA cm−2, and FF was 73.57%. The device performance was significantly improved when C-dots were used as the cathode interlayer. The current density versus voltage (J-V) curves of OSCs with different concentration of C-dots were demonstrated in Fig. S4 and Table S1. The device based on the optimal condition of C-dots cathode interlayer showed PCEmax of 15.41%, where Voc was 0.83 V, Jsc was 24.52 mA cm−2, and FF was 75.89%. It is worth noting that there is no thermal annealing required for the C-dots interlayer, while a 200 °C thermal annealing treatment is required for the ZnO interlayer.

Meanwhile, we also made devices with PEI as the cathode interlayer, as shown in Fig. 2a and Table 1. The PEI based devices exhibited very poor device performance with a Voc, Jsc, FF, and PCE value of 0.79 V, 19.43 mA cm−2, 61.14%, and 9.47%, respectively. The result was similar to the previous report in the literature [25]. The result showed that although PEI could be used as a cathode interlayer for fullerene based OSCs, it was not applicable for high efficiency non-fullerene based OSCs due to adverse chemical reactions between PEI and non-fullerene receptors [26]. Fig. S6 showed the Y6 solution and the mixture of Y6 with C-dots solution, respectively. The color of the Y6 did not change after adding the C-dots solution. Meanwhile, the absorption spectra of Y6 with C-dots did not significantly change compared to the Y6 solution. The result suggested that there is no reaction between C-dots and Y6. Therefore, the C-dots, as the cathode interface layer, were conducive to improve the efficiency and stability of OSCs. Moreover, PEI is an insulating material, generally only as an interface modification layer, and can not be done alone as the electron transport layer to effectively extract electron and block holes. The argument was supported by the dark J-V curves of PEI-based device (Fig. S5).

Fig. 2b showed the external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectrum of OSCs with different cathode interlayer. The integrated Jsc data from EQE measurements were 23.25, 23.43 and 18.99 mA cm−2 for ZnO, C-dots and PEI based device, respectively, which were well consistent with Jsc derived from the J-V characteristic (deviation within 5%). Because of the poor performance for the PEI based device, we focused our discussion on ZnO and C-dots based devices in the following study. The transmittance spectra of Glass/ITO and Glass/ITO/C-dots were listed in Fig. S7. The high transmittance allowed the organic active layer to capture photons well. The intrinsic defect of ZnO cathode interlayer caused a lot of traps in both the bulk and interface, which would play as the recombination center, resulting in the degenerative device performance. The corresponding J-V characteristics of devices under dark conditions were shown in Fig. 2c. Obviously, device with C-dots cathode interlayer showed a higher rectification ratio and a lower leakage current than device with ZnO interlayer, indicating that C-dots-based cathode interlayer can improve the carrier extraction efficiency and reduce the recombination of charges.

In order to study the electronic properties of the cathode interlayers, ultraviolet photo-electron spectroscopy (UPS) was used to investigate the WF of ITO, ITO/ZnO and ITO/C-dots, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The WF of ITO, ITO/ZnO and ITO/C-dots were 4.71 eV, 4.26 eV, 4.20 eV, respectively, indicating that C-dots can efficiently reduce the WF of ITO and allow for more efficient extraction of electrons. Furthermore, the morphology of the different interlayers was detected by atomic force microscopy (AFM). From Fig. S8, the root-mean-square (RMS) of ITO, ITO/ZnO, ITO/C-dots were 4.13 nm, 2.17 nm, 3.33 nm, respectively. After modification with ZnO, we find that the roughness of ITO substrate decreases and becomes relatively smooth. The roughness of ITO substrate modified by C-dots was also reduced, but the modification ability was not as strong as ZnO. It is because that the C-dots interlayer is too thin to fill with the rough structure of ITO film effectively. AFM morphology of the PM6:Y6 film on different interlayers was shown in Fig. S9. A uniform film facilitates the formation of better interface contact, reduces internal defects and inhibits the recombination of electron-holes. Then, we tested the contact angle of different interlayers with two kinds of solutions with different polarities, and calculated the surface energy with the Owen method [27], as shown in Fig. S10 and Table S2. The higher surface energy of C-dots indicates better wettability and better compatibility with the active layer.

To investigate the carrier transport property of the interlayer, we tested the electron mobility (μ
e ) by using electron-only devices with space-charge-limited current (SCLC) method and the μ
e can be calculated by the following formula [28]: J = (9/8)ε
0
ε
r
μ
e ((V 2)/(d 3)), where J is the current density, ε
0 is the dielectric constant in vacuum, ε
r is the dielectric constant of the material, V represents the applied voltage potential, and d is the thickness of the active layer (100 nm). As shown in Fig. 3b, the electron mobility of the electron-only devices (ITO/interlayer/PM6:Y6/PDINN/Al) based on ZnO or C-dots interlayer were 2.69x10−4 (cm2 V−1 s−1) and 3.22x10−4 (cm2 V−1 s−1), respectively. It turns out that there is good interfacial compatibility between C-dots and active layer, which promotes charge transfer and improves the electron mobility. Fig. S11 displayed Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement of devices based on different cathode interlayer, which were recorded at VOC condition under 1 standard simulated sun illumination (AM 1.5G at 100 mW cm−2). The Nyquist plots results were fitted by employing an equivalent circuit model (inset in Fig. S11). As depicted, the device based on C-dots showed small the surface resistance (RS) and the charge transfer resistance (RCT), indicting that the interface defect is small and the charge recombination is effectively suppressed.

In addition, Fig. 3c showed the J
ph -V
eff curves to study the ability of exciton dissociation, which J
ph and V
eff satisfy the following relationship [29]: J
ph = J
L -J
D , V
eff = V
0 -V
a , where J
L and J
D are photocurrent density and dark current density respectively, V
0 is the voltage when J
ph = 0 and V
a is the applied voltage. Exciton dissociation efficiency P(E,T) is defined as J
ph /J
sat under the short-circuit condition [30]. J
sat is the saturation current density. The C-dots-based device had a higher P(E,T) of 97.8% than the ZnO-based device (97.2%). The higher P(E,T) value for C-dots-based device indicates that the excitons can be more effectively dissociated into charge carriers，leading to an increased Jsc and a high FF. To study the effect of interlayer film on the recombination process of photogenerated carriers, the relationship between Jsc and light-intensity (Plight) was measured. According to the law of Jsc∝(Plight)S, when S approaches 1, the biomolecular recombination in the device is very weak [31]. In Fig. 3d, compared to the ZnO-based device (S = 0.95), the slope of the C-dots-based device was closer to 1 (S = 0.98), likely to inhibit the bimolecular recombination.

Finally, the photostability of the devices were investigated, which played an important role for production and commercialization of OSCs. The absorbed UV light induces the oxygen desorption that could result in “photoinduced shunts” issue and increased carrier recombination loss [32,33]. The dark J-V characteristics before and after 1 h illumination displayed in Fig. 4 a–b. After 1 h UV irradiation, the reverse current density of the ZnO-based device was an order of magnitude higher, indicating a serious “photoinduced shunts” issue. In contrast, the device with C-dots interlayer exhibited almost unchanged dark J-V characteristics after 1 h UV irradiation. We also showed the absorption spectral of active layer on different interlayer under 1 h illumination in Fig. S12. There was no obvious change of C-dots compared to the ZnO, indicating that the stable contact between C-dots and Y6. Furthermore, ZnO is known as a photocatalyst for organic compound, which can facilitate the decomposition of active layers under UV illumination via the photocatalytic reaction [34]. Fig. 4c–d showed the evolution of the J-V characteristics of the device with different cathode interlayers under continuous UV illumination (provided by a UV lamp at 5 mW cm−2). As shown in Fig. 4e–f, the performance of the ZnO-based device degraded fast, with maintaining only 80.9% of their initial PCE after 60 min continuous illumination, accompanied by a substantial drop in all photovoltaic parameters. The severe degradation was mainly attributed to the photocatalytic properties and “photoinduced shunts” issue of ZnO. On the contrary, the device with C-dots cathode interlayer demonstrated greatly improved photostability and 97.5% initial efficiency was maintained under the same condition.

In summary, we synthesized an aqueous solution treated and inexpensive C-dots as cathode interlayer for efficient and stable OSCs. The C-dots with amino groups synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal reaction had excellent photoelectric properties, allowing it to reduce the work function, facilitate the transport and collection of the photogenerated charge carriers, and reduce the recombination of charge carrier, leading to the improvement of Jsc and FF in device. As a result, the PCE reached 15.41% with PM6: Y6 as active layers. At the same time, under 1 h of continuous UV illumination, the C-dots based device could maintain the initial efficiency of 97.5%, while ZnO could only maintain the efficiency of 80.9%. The photochemical stability of C-dots overcomes the inevitable defects of ZnO. Therefore, it demonstrates that C-dots are a promising electron transport layer material for the commercialization and large-scale production of OSCs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019B151502060), Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (No.2019050001), Guangzhou Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (202102020672).
